quantum-physics.title: Quantum Physics
quantum-physics.content:
Quantum physics, also known as quantum mechanics, is the fundamental theory in physics that provides a description of the physical properties of nature at the scale of atoms and subatomic particles. It is the foundation of all quantum physics including quantum chemistry, quantum field theory, quantum technology, and quantum information science.

### 1. The Wave-Particle Duality
At the heart of quantum mechanics lies the principle that every particle or quantum entity may be described as either a particle or a wave. This is encapsulated by the de Broglie relation, which connects momentum \(p\) to wavelength \(\lambda\) via Planck's constant \(h\): \[\lambda = \frac{h}{p}\]. This duality challenges classical intuitions about localized matter.

### 2. The Schrödinger Equation
The state of a quantum system is described by a wave function, denoted by \(\Psi(r, t)\). Its evolution over time is governed by the time-dependent Schrödinger equation: \[i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \Psi(r, t) = \left[ -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m} \nabla^2 + V(r, t) \right] \Psi(r, t)\]. This equation is the corner stone of non-relativistic quantum mechanics, predicting the probabilities of finding a particle in a given region of space.

### 3. Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
One of the most profound implications of quantum theory is the existence of fundamental limits to the precision with which certain pairs of physical properties, such as position \(x\) and momentum \(p\), can be known simultaneously: \[\Delta x \Delta p \ge \frac{\hbar}{2}\]. This is not a limitation of measurement apparatus, but an intrinsic feature of quantum systems.

### 4. Quantum Superposition and Entanglement
Quantum systems can exist in multiple states simultaneously—a phenomenon known as superposition. When two or more particles interact such that their individual quantum states are dependent on each other, they become entangled, exhibiting correlations that defy classical locality, as famously discussed in the EPR paradox.

### 5. Quantization of Energy
Unlike classical systems where energy can take any continuous value, bound quantum systems often have discrete energy levels. The energy of a harmonic oscillator, for instance, is quantized according to: \[E_n = \hbar \omega \left( n + \frac{1}{2} \right)\], where \(n\) is a non-negative integer.

### 6. Applications and Modern Interpretation
From the development of the transistor and the laser to the emerging field of quantum computing, quantum mechanics has paved the way for transformative technologies. Interpretational frameworks, such as the Copenhagen interpretation or the Many-Worlds interpretation, continue to be subjects of deep philosophical and physical inquiry.

formulas.title: Schrödinger Equation
formulas.breakdown: The equation defines the evolution of the wave function \(\Psi\) under the influence of the Hamiltonian operator \(\hat{H}\).

formulas.title: De Broglie Wavelength
formulas.breakdown: Relates the wavelength \(\lambda\) to the momentum \(p\) of a particle via Planck's constant \(h\).

formulas.title: Uncertainty Principle
formulas.breakdown: Quantifies the fundamental limit of precision in simultaneous knowledge of position and momentum.
